sections.markdown:
# Test Systems and IN-ORDER-TO

A project usually has tests that depend on the project itself plus a test framework (`fiveam`, `parachute`, `rove`, ...). The convention is two systems:

* `cool` — the library.
* `cool/tests` (or `cool-tests`) — depends on `cool` and a test framework. `(asdf:test-system :cool)` should run them.

The glue is the `:in-order-to` clause on the main system, telling ASDF to fan out the `test-op` to the tests system:

```lisp
(asdf:defsystem #:cool
  :depends-on (#:alexandria)
  :serial t
  :components ((:file "package") (:file "core"))
  :in-order-to ((asdf:test-op (asdf:test-op #:cool/tests))))

(asdf:defsystem #:cool/tests
  :depends-on (#:cool #:fiveam)
  :serial t
  :components ((:file "tests"))
  :perform (asdf:test-op (op c)
             (uiop:symbol-call :fiveam :run! :cool-suite)))
```

Reading the keys:

* `:in-order-to ((TEST-OP (TEST-OP #:other)))` — "to do test-op on me, do test-op on `other`".
* `:perform (OP (op c) BODY)` — define what running operation `OP` on this component actually means. We use `uiop:symbol-call` so the test framework symbol is resolved at runtime, after `fiveam` is loaded.

This pattern keeps the test framework out of your library's runtime dependencies — production users don't pull in `fiveam`.

sections.explain: DEFSYSTEM accepts the :IN-ORDER-TO clause. We register the system and confirm FIND-SYSTEM finds it.
TEST-OP is a subclass of ASDF:OPERATION. SUBTYPEP returns T (and a definitive second value).
Sanity-check that ASDF:TEST-OP exists as an exported symbol.

sections.prompt: Define a system NAMED-TEST-SYS (with no components and no test redirection — just a stub). Then define HAS-IN-ORDER-TO-P (SYS) returning T iff system SYS has any :IN-ORDER-TO entries (i.e. the result of asdf:component-in-order-to is non-NIL). For our stub the answer is NIL.
